title: 3D Genomics Analysis
introduction:
OpenPretext includes a suite of 3D genomics analysis tools that reveal structural properties of the genome beyond what's visible in the raw contact map. These analyses help you assess assembly quality, detect misassemblies, and understand chromatin organization.

In this lesson, you'll learn to:
• Run the core analysis pipeline (insulation, P(s) decay, compartments)
• Read and interpret analysis tracks and metrics
• Understand the composite health score
• Normalize the contact matrix with ICE
• Export analysis results for external use

We'll use the koala genome, which has a clean chromosome structure ideal for demonstrating these tools.

steps.title: Open the Sidebar
steps.instruction: First, open the sidebar by pressing **I** or clicking the **Info** button in the toolbar. Scroll down to find the **3D Analysis** section. This is your analysis command center — it contains compute buttons, result displays, and export options.
steps.hint: The 3D Analysis section is near the bottom of the sidebar, below Assembly Metrics and History.

steps.title: Run the Core Analysis Pipeline
steps.instruction:
Click **Compute All** to run all three core analyses simultaneously:

• **Insulation score** — measures how well each genomic region is insulated from its neighbors (detects TAD boundaries)
• **P(s) decay** — measures how contact frequency drops with genomic distance (indicates genome compaction)
• **A/B compartments** — identifies active (A) and inactive (B) chromatin compartments

All three run in a background Web Worker so the UI stays responsive. Watch for the results to appear below the buttons.
steps.hint: The computation may take a few seconds. Buttons are disabled during computation to prevent conflicts.

steps.title: Read the Health Score
steps.instruction:
After computation, a **Health Score** card appears showing a composite 0–100 score. This combines five components:

• **N50** (20%) — contiguity, how large your contigs are
• **P(s)** (25%) — contact decay quality, how well the power-law fits
• **Int** (20%) — assembly integrity, fewer misassembly flags is better
• **A/B** (15%) — compartment strength, how distinct A and B compartments are
• **Lib** (20%) — library quality (cis/trans ratio), requires scaffold assignments

A score above 70 indicates a well-assembled genome. The koala should score reasonably well. Note which components are strong and which are weak.
steps.hint: Hover over the component abbreviations to see their full names and scores.

steps.title: Understand the P(s) Decay Chart
steps.instruction:
Below the health score, find the **P(s) decay chart** — a log-log plot showing contact frequency vs genomic distance. Key things to look for:

• The **exponent** (slope) should be between -1.0 and -1.5 for a well-compacted genome
• The **R²** value indicates how well the power-law fits — above 0.9 is good
• The **range** shows how many distance bins were used for fitting

You can click **Snapshot Baseline** to save the current P(s) curve. After curation operations, a new curve will overlay the baseline so you can see if your edits improved compaction.
steps.hint: Shallow exponents (closer to 0) suggest poor compaction or misassembly. Steep exponents (closer to -2) suggest over-corrected data.

steps.title: Examine Analysis Tracks
steps.instruction:
The analysis results are also rendered as **tracks** along the edges of the contact map. Toggle tracks on with **X** or the **Tracks** button. You should see:

• **Insulation Score** (green line) — dips indicate TAD boundaries
• **TAD Boundaries** (red markers) — detected boundary positions
• **A/B Compartments** (blue-yellow-red heatmap) — blue = B (inactive), red = A (active)

Zoom into a chromosome to see how these tracks relate to the contact map structure.
steps.hint: Compare the insulation dips with the visual boundaries between bright contact blocks in the map.

steps.title: Normalize with ICE
steps.instruction:
Contact maps have systematic biases (GC content, mappability, restriction site density). **ICE normalization** corrects these biases by iteratively balancing row and column sums.

Click **Normalize (ICE)** to run the normalization. After it completes, notice:
• A new 'ICE Bias' track appears showing per-bin correction factors
• The P(s) curve and compartments are automatically recomputed on the normalized data
• The P(s) exponent may change — this is the bias-corrected value
steps.hint: ICE typically runs 10-50 iterations. Bins with very low coverage are masked (excluded from normalization).

steps.title: Export Analysis Data
steps.instruction:
Analysis results can be exported for use in external tools (R, Python, IGV). Scroll to the bottom of the 3D Analysis section to find export buttons:

• **Export Insulation** — BedGraph format, loadable in genome browsers
• **Export P(s)** — TSV format with distance and contact frequency columns
• **Export Compartments** — BedGraph format with eigenvector values

These files use genomic coordinates derived from your contig ordering and sizes.
steps.hint: BedGraph files can be loaded back into OpenPretext via the Load Track button to compare analyses across different sessions.

steps.title: Analysis Summary
steps.instruction:
You've learned the core analysis workflow:

1. **Compute All** runs insulation, P(s), and compartments in parallel
2. The **health score** gives a quick quality overview
3. The **P(s) chart** shows genome compaction quality with baseline comparison
4. **Analysis tracks** overlay results on the contact map for visual inspection
5. **ICE normalization** corrects systematic biases
6. **Export** saves results for external analysis

In your curation workflow, run analysis early to identify problems, curate to fix them, and re-run analysis to verify improvement. The health score is your compass — aim to increase it with each round of curation.
